APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem—document clustering using word embeddings and Wasserstein distance—and proposes scalable algorithmic improvements (modified B-ADMM, Elkan-style pruning, parallelization) that are directly relevant to the community. It evaluates against standard baselines (Tf-idf, LDA, PV, Kusner et al.) across multiple well-known datasets (20Newsgroups, Reuters, BBC, Ohsumed) using accepted metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure), and provides code and hyperparameter details. The formulation (documents as discrete distributions over embeddings and D2‑clustering) and thorough empirical comparisons make it appropriate and well positioned for a top-tier NLP/ML venue. | B(5): The paper addresses document clustering using word embeddings and Wasserstein (EMD) distance, a topic squarely within NLP/ML research on representation and unsupervised learning. It proposes algorithmic contributions (modified B-ADMM, Elkan-style pruning, parallelization) and demonstrates empirical gains across standard datasets (20Newsgroups, Reuters, BBC, Ohsumed, etc.) using common metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure), matching the methodological and evaluation standards expected at top venues. Code and hyperparameters are provided and related work coverage is adequate, indicating the work is positioned correctly for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem—document clustering using word embeddings and Wasserstein distance—and proposes scalable algorithmic improvements (modified B-ADMM, Elkan-style pruning, parallelization) that are directly relevant to the community. It evaluates against standard baselines (Tf-idf, LDA, PV, Kusner et al.) across multiple well-known datasets (20Newsgroups, Reuters, BBC, Ohsumed) using accepted metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure), and provides code and hyperparameter details. The formulation (documents as discrete distributions over embeddings and D2‑clustering) and thorough empirical comparisons make it appropriate and well positioned for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses document clustering using word embeddings and Wasserstein (EMD) distance, a topic squarely within NLP/ML research on representation and unsupervised learning. It proposes algorithmic contributions (modified B-ADMM, Elkan-style pruning, parallelization) and demonstrates empirical gains across standard datasets (20Newsgroups, Reuters, BBC, Ohsumed, etc.) using common metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure), matching the methodological and evaluation standards expected at top venues. Code and hyperparameters are provided and related work coverage is adequate, indicating the work is positioned correctly for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—scalable, semantics-aware document clustering—by representing documents as discrete distributions over word embeddings and clustering with Wasserstein (EMD) distances, which is a conceptually strong alternative to traditional bag-of-words or centroid methods. The engineering and algorithmic contributions (modified Bregman ADMM for barycenters, Elkan-style pruning, and parallelization) materially make D2-clustering practical, and the empirical claim that it consistently outperforms baselines (Tf-idf, LDA, PV, etc.) and reaches >90% of best AMI across datasets indicates likely adoption by researchers. While the method still incurs nontrivial runtime and needs multi-processor parallelization and domain-specific embeddings in some cases, these are implementation/engineering hurdles rather than conceptual limitations, so the work should open follow-on research on scalable optimal-transport clustering and see substantive use in the field. | B(4): The paper tackles the important problem of scalable, semantically informed document clustering by representing documents as discrete distributions over word embeddings and clustering with 2-Wasserstein (D2) distance. By making barycenter computation practical via a modified Bregman ADMM algorithm, Elkan-style pruning and parallelization, it converts a previously computationally prohibitive method into a usable tool, and empirical results show consistent gains over strong baselines across multiple datasets. These advances are likely to change how researchers approach embedding-based clustering and spawn follow-up work on optimal-transport methods and efficient barycenter algorithms, though remaining runtime and domain-embedding dependencies limit it from being fully transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—scalable, semantics-aware document clustering—by representing documents as discrete distributions over word embeddings and clustering with Wasserstein (EMD) distances, which is a conceptually strong alternative to traditional bag-of-words or centroid methods. The engineering and algorithmic contributions (modified Bregman ADMM for barycenters, Elkan-style pruning, and parallelization) materially make D2-clustering practical, and the empirical claim that it consistently outperforms baselines (Tf-idf, LDA, PV, etc.) and reaches >90% of best AMI across datasets indicates likely adoption by researchers. While the method still incurs nontrivial runtime and needs multi-processor parallelization and domain-specific embeddings in some cases, these are implementation/engineering hurdles rather than conceptual limitations, so the work should open follow-on research on scalable optimal-transport clustering and see substantive use in the field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles the important problem of scalable, semantically informed document clustering by representing documents as discrete distributions over word embeddings and clustering with 2-Wasserstein (D2) distance. By making barycenter computation practical via a modified Bregman ADMM algorithm, Elkan-style pruning and parallelization, it converts a previously computationally prohibitive method into a usable tool, and empirical results show consistent gains over strong baselines across multiple datasets. These advances are likely to change how researchers approach embedding-based clustering and spawn follow-up work on optimal-transport methods and efficient barycenter algorithms, though remaining runtime and domain-embedding dependencies limit it from being fully transformative.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): Experiments are broad and thorough, evaluating the method against many strong baselines across multiple datasets (BBCNews, Wiki events, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, Ohsumed, etc.) using multiple clustering metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure, etc.). Sensitivity analyses (support size m and number of clusters K) and algorithmic variants (B-ADMM, Elkan-style pruning, parallelization) are reported, and hyperparameters plus code availability support reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as adequate and the paper explicitly discusses limitations (runtime, need for domain-specific embeddings, constrained Ohsumed experiments), indicating balanced reporting of negative results and failure modes. | B(4): The paper evaluates the method across multiple heterogeneous datasets (BBCNews, Wiki events, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, BBCSports, Ohsumed) and against a wide set of baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, LDA, NMF, PV, spectral, LSI, Kusner et al.), using standard clustering metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure), which supports the empirical claims. Sensitivity analyses are reported for support size m and cluster number K, and implementation details (modified B-ADMM, Elkan pruning, parallelization), code, and hyperparameters are provided to aid reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as adequate and some limitations/failure modes are acknowledged (runtime costs, limited Ohsumed experiments, need for domain-specific embeddings). Overall the evaluation is thorough but not exhaustive—runtime and domain-specific performance merit deeper analysis—so the paper earns a solid but not perfect score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by extensive experiments across multiple datasets and baselines (Tf-idf, LDA, NMF, PV, Kusner et al.), with code and hyperparameters provided. The algorithmic contribution (modified Bregman ADMM, Elkan-style pruning, parallelization) is plausible and makes D2-clustering practical, but the summary does not indicate formal proofs of convergence or full theoretical analysis of the modified solver. Experimental methodology is generally sound (multiple datasets and metrics), though the summary does not report statistical significance testing or detailed ablation to fully rule out tuning/baseline issues. Overall, results justify the conclusions with only minor methodological and theoretical gaps. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and backed by extensive empirical evidence: multiple datasets (BBCNews, Wiki events, Reuters, 20Newsgroups, BBCSports, Ohsumed), a wide set of baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, LDA, NMF, PV, Kusner et al.), and standard clustering metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure) support the reported gains. The proposed algorithmic contributions (modified Bregman ADMM for Wasserstein barycenters, Elkan-style pruning, parallelization) plausibly address prior scalability limits, and runtime/hyperparameter details plus released code strengthen reproducibility. Minor technical concerns remain: the summary does not indicate formal proofs or full derivation completeness for the modified ADMM or statistical significance testing of results, and one dataset (Ohsumed) had limited experiments — these do not invalidate the core claims but warrant clarification. Overall, the work is technically solid with only modest gaps in theoretical exposition and statistical reporting.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper combines existing ideas (representing documents as distributions over word embeddings and using Wasserstein/EMD for comparison, cf. Kusner et al.) with substantial, nontrivial algorithmic contributions: a modified Bregman ADMM to compute Wasserstein barycenters, Elkan-style pruning, and a parallel implementation that make D2-clustering practical at scale. The authors clearly separate these engineering/algorithmic improvements from prior work and demonstrate consistent empirical gains across datasets, so the contribution is a notable and useful advance rather than purely incremental. | B(4): The paper adapts the idea of representing documents as discrete distributions over word embeddings and clustering under the 2-Wasserstein (D2-clustering) framework—an approach related to prior embedding-based distances (e.g., Kusner et al. 2015)—but the main novelty is the nontrivial algorithmic advance: a modified Bregman ADMM for computing Wasserstein barycenters plus Elkan-style pruning and parallelization that makes D2-clustering practical at scale. The authors explicitly differentiate from prior methods (including the KNN-graph WMD approach) and provide empirical evidence and code, so the contribution is a notable and clearly articulated extension rather than a wholly new paradigm.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines existing ideas (representing documents as distributions over word embeddings and using Wasserstein/EMD for comparison, cf. The authors clearly separate these engineering/algorithmic improvements from prior work and demonstrate consistent empirical gains across datasets, so the contribution is a notable and useful advance rather than purely incremental.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear flow from method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (D2-clustering with B-ADMM, scalability improvements, empirical gains) are clearly stated early. Notation and methodology are presented clearly enough to report hyperparameters, per-iteration runtimes, and a wide set of evaluation metrics and baselines, and no major notational ambiguities are reported in the summary. Figures/tables/examples appear informative (datasets, metrics, runtimes and comparisons are documented), and the writing quality is adequate overall, suggesting only minor presentation issues remain. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a logical flow (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and its main contributions are stated early (scalable D2-clustering over word-embedding distributions, modified B-ADMM, and parallel/Elkan optimizations). The notation and methodological description are presented clearly enough to follow (documents as discrete measures, 2-Wasserstein objective, barycenter computation), and the experimental material (datasets, baselines, metrics) suggests figures/tables and examples are informative. Writing is generally adequate, though a few presentation points—e.g., discussion of runtime limitations and domain-specific embedding needs—could be clarified further, hence minor presentation issues rather than major flaws.

The notation and methodological description are presented clearly enough to follow (documents as discrete measures, 2-Wasserstein objective, barycenter computation), and the experimental material (datasets, baselines, metrics) suggests figures/tables and examples are informative. MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper compares against a broad set of relevant baselines including traditional (Tf-idf, LSI, LDA, NMF), embedding-based methods (AvgDoc, Paragraph Vectors) and a closely related Wasserstein/KNN-graph approach (Kusner et al., 2015). Comparisons use the same datasets and standard clustering metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure, etc.), with hyperparameters reported and code released, supporting fairness. The authors acknowledge limitations (e.g., Ohsumed experiments limited, need for domain-specific embeddings) and discuss scalability trade-offs and algorithmic differences (B-ADMM, Elkan pruning, sensitivity analyses), and related work coverage is described as adequate. | B(5): The paper compares against a wide and relevant set of baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, LDA, NMF, PV, LSI, spectral methods, Kusner et al. 2015 KNN-graph, etc.) across multiple standard datasets (20Newsgroups, Reuters, BBC, Ohsumed, Wiki events) using common metrics (AMI, ARI, V-measure, etc.). Comparisons appear fair: same data and evaluation metrics, hyperparameters are reported and code is available, and the authors discuss prior-method limitations (e.g., scalability of Kusner et al. and heavy LPs in original D2-clustering) and analyze efficiency differences introduced by their B-ADMM and pruning/parallelization. Given this breadth and the documented experimental setup, the comparisons are comprehensive and appropriate.

Given this breadth and the documented experimental setup, the comparisons are comprehensive and appropriate.

main_claims: Propose a scalable nonparametric document clustering framework that represents documents as discrete distributions over word embeddings and clusters them using Wasserstein (EMD) distance and D2-clustering.
Use a modified Bregman ADMM algorithm (B-ADMM) with parallelization and Elkan-style pruning to compute Wasserstein barycenters efficiently, making D2-clustering practical for document analysis.

Empirically, D2-clustering consistently outperforms several baselines (Tf-idf, AvgDoc, LDA, NMF, PV) across tasks and datasets, and is reported as the only method reaching more than 90% of the best AMI on each dataset.
The approach is robust across varying document lengths and domains; the support size m has limited sensitivity while the number of clusters K has larger effect.
_summary.methodology: Represent each document as a discrete probability measure over word-embedding support points and perform D2-clustering by minimizing within-cluster sum of squared 2-Wasserstein distances; compute cluster barycenters via a modified Bregman ADMM algorithm with K-means-like assignment steps and use Elkan pruning and parallel implementation for efficiency.
limitations: Original D2-clustering required large-scale LPs and was computationally heavy; efficiency improvements are needed for practicality (historical limitation).
The modified Kusner et al. (2015) KNN-graph based method is stated to have limited scalability.
Experiments on the Ohsumed dataset are limited; authors note a need for domain-specific embeddings to better capture domain knowledge.
The method still incurs nontrivial runtime and requires multi-processor parallelization for large datasets (per-iteration times reported).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - document clustering using Wasserstein distance / word embeddings
